sections.name: Section II: Create Performance Task — Written Response
sections.questions: 2 written responses (4 prompts: WR1, WR2a, WR2b, WR2c)
sections.notes: Program code, video, and Personalized Project Reference submitted via portfolio; at least 9 hours of in-class time

exam_overview.pseudocode_language: College Board pseudocode (not Python or Java)
tips: All code on the AP CSP exam uses College Board pseudocode — never Python, Java, or any other real language
5 of the 70 MCQs reference a provided reading passage about a computing innovation
8 of the 70 MCQs are multi-select — you must choose exactly 2 correct answers
Big Idea 3 (Algorithms and Programming) is the heaviest unit at ~30–35% of the exam — prioritize pseudocode tracing practice
The Create Performance Task accounts for 30% of your score — start early and save your program code and video

key_notes: Lists are 1-based — aList[1] is the first element, not aList[0]
REPEAT UNTIL runs until the condition is TRUE (loop stops when condition is met)
RANDOM(a, b) returns a random integer from a to b inclusive

key_topics: Types of computing innovations (physical, nonphysical software, nonphysical concept)
Iterative and incremental development process (investigate → design → prototype → test)
Collaboration: diverse perspectives reduce bias; pair programming model
Program inputs and outputs (tactile, audio, visual, text; user or other programs)
Event-driven vs. sequential programming
Three types of errors: syntax, logic, run-time
Debugging strategies: test cases, hand tracing, display statements
Documentation: code comments, intellectual property, Creative Commons licensing
learning_objectives: CRD-2.A: Describe the purpose of a computing innovation
CRD-2.B/C/D: Explain how a program functions and identify its inputs and outputs
CRD-2.E/F: Develop a program using an iterative design process
CRD-2.I: Identify and correct errors in algorithms and programs
CRD-1.A/B: Explain how collaboration improves computing innovations
common_question_types: Classify a given innovation as physical, nonphysical software, or nonphysical concept
Identify the type of error (syntax vs. logic vs. run-time) in a described scenario
Determine which development step (investigate, design, prototype, test) is being performed
Explain how collaboration or diverse perspectives would improve a specific program
exam_tips: Know the three error types cold: syntax = won't run; logic = runs but wrong result; run-time = crashes during execution
Event-driven programming responds to triggers (clicks, key presses); sequential runs top-to-bottom in fixed order
Iterative development means repeating cycles of design-prototype-test, not doing them once in order

key_topics: Binary number system: 1 bit = 2 values; N bits = 2^N values
Overflow error (exceeds storage capacity) and round-off error (loss of precision)
Analog vs. digital data; sampling to convert analog → digital
Lossless compression (exact reconstruction, e.g., run-length encoding) vs. lossy (approximate, e.g., JPEG, MP3)
Metadata: data about data (file size, date, location)
Data collection, cleaning, and bias (systematic errors in data)
Correlation vs. causation in data analysis
Big data, citizen science, crowdsourcing, open data
learning_objectives: DAT-1.A/B/C: Explain how data is represented in bits and the effects of encoding/abstraction
DAT-2.A/B: Describe what information can be extracted from data and metadata
DAT-2.C/D/E: Explain and demonstrate how programs extract insight from data
common_question_types: Calculate how many values N bits can represent (answer: 2^N)
Distinguish lossless from lossy compression and identify use cases for each
Identify what metadata reveals about a person or file
Explain why a dataset has bias or why correlation does not imply causation
exam_tips: 2^N is the formula for number of values representable with N bits — memorize it
Lossless = exact reconstruction (text files, program code); lossy = smaller file but data lost (images, audio, video)
Metadata can reveal location, time, and behavior even when the content itself is private — a common privacy exam angle

units.code_intensity: very high — almost entirely pseudocode tracing
key_topics: Variables and assignment: a ← expression (copies value; does NOT create a link)
Sequencing, selection (IF/ELSE), iteration (REPEAT n TIMES, REPEAT UNTIL, FOR EACH)
Lists: 1-based indexing; APPEND, INSERT, REMOVE, LENGTH operations
Boolean expressions: AND, OR, NOT; relational operators
Procedures: parameters, arguments, RETURN; procedural abstraction hides complexity
Data abstraction: using lists to manage groups of related values
Linear search (any list, O(n)) vs. binary search (sorted list required, O(log n))
Algorithm efficiency: polynomial time = reasonable; exponential/factorial = unreasonable
Heuristic solutions for hard problems; undecidable problems (e.g., Halting Problem)
Simulations: model real-world processes; use RANDOM for probabilistic behavior
learning_objectives: AAP-1.A/B/C/D: Represent, assign, and abstract values in variables and lists
AAP-2.A–P: Trace sequencing, selection, iteration, and list operations in pseudocode
AAP-3.A–G: Call and develop procedures; use libraries, random values, and simulations
AAP-4.A/B: Distinguish reasonable from unreasonable time; explain undecidable problems
common_question_types: Trace a CB pseudocode segment and determine the final value of a variable or output
Identify which code segment produces the same result as a given segment (code equivalence)
Determine the minimum number of comparisons binary search needs for a list of size n
Classify an algorithm as reasonable (polynomial) or unreasonable (exponential) time
exam_tips: Lists are 1-based in CB pseudocode — aList[1] is the first element, not aList[0]
REPEAT UNTIL(condition) stops when the condition becomes TRUE — don't confuse this with REPEAT WHILE
Binary search requires a sorted list and takes at most log₂(n) comparisons; the Halting Problem is the canonical undecidable problem
Two code segments are equivalent only if they produce identical output for ALL possible inputs, not just one test case
pseudocode_tracing_reminders: Assignment: a ← 5 stores 5 in a; a ← a + 1 increments a by 1
IF(condition) { ... } ELSE { ... } — only the matching branch executes
REPEAT n TIMES executes the block exactly n times regardless of what happens inside
REPEAT UNTIL(condition) checks condition AFTER each iteration (like a do-while)
FOR EACH item IN aList iterates through every element left-to-right
APPEND(aList, value) adds to the END; INSERT(aList, i, value) inserts at position i

key_topics: Network basics: IP address, router, protocol, bandwidth
Packet switching: data split into packets, sent independently, reassembled at destination
Key protocols: TCP (reliable delivery), HTTP/HTTPS (web), DNS (URL → IP translation)
Internet vs. World Wide Web (Internet is the infrastructure; WWW is web pages over HTTP)
Fault tolerance and redundancy: multiple paths ensure delivery even when components fail
Parallel computing: simultaneous processing on multiple processors; reduces total time
Distributed computing: tasks spread across multiple computers; enables larger problems
Speedup limits: maximum speedup cannot exceed number of processors; some tasks cannot be parallelized due to dependencies
learning_objectives: CSN-1.A/B/C/D: Explain how the Internet works, including protocols and communication
CSN-1.E: Explain how redundancy makes the Internet fault tolerant
CSN-2.A/B: Describe benefits and limitations of parallel and distributed computing
common_question_types: Explain why the Internet is fault tolerant (packet switching + multiple paths)
Identify which protocol handles a specific function (DNS, TCP, HTTP, HTTPS)
Determine whether a parallel computing scenario will improve performance and by how much
Distinguish between parallel computing (same machine, multiple processors) and distributed computing (multiple machines)
exam_tips: The Internet is fault tolerant because packets can take different routes — removing one node does not break the whole network
DNS translates domain names (like google.com) to IP addresses — think of it as the Internet's phone book
Parallel speedup is limited: if 10% of a program must run sequentially, you can never achieve more than 10x speedup no matter how many processors you add

key_topics: Beneficial and harmful effects of computing innovations; unintended consequences
Digital divide: unequal access based on socioeconomic status, geography, disability
Computing bias: reflected in training data, algorithms, and design choices
Crowdsourcing and citizen science: using many people to solve large problems
Intellectual property: copyright, open-source, Creative Commons licensing
PII (Personally Identifiable Information) and privacy risks from data collection
Security threats: phishing, keylogging, malware, rogue access points
Encryption: symmetric (same key) vs. public-key/asymmetric (public encrypts, private decrypts)
Multi-factor authentication (MFA); HTTPS and SSL/TLS for secure web communication
Ethical concerns: automation and job displacement, algorithmic fairness, surveillance
learning_objectives: IOC-1.A/B: Explain beneficial and harmful effects including unintended consequences
IOC-1.C/D: Describe the digital divide and computing bias
IOC-1.E/F: Explain crowdsourcing and legal/ethical concerns
IOC-2.A/C/D: Describe privacy risks, malicious actors, and secure transmission elements
common_question_types: Identify an unintended consequence or harmful effect of a described computing innovation
Explain how a specific attack (phishing, keylogging, malware) compromises security
Distinguish symmetric from public-key encryption and explain when each is used
Explain why a specific dataset or algorithm might be biased
exam_tips: Almost every computing innovation has BOTH beneficial and harmful effects — exam questions often ask for both sides
PII questions are common: combining non-PII data points can create PII (e.g., ZIP code + birth date + gender can identify an individual)
Public key encryption: the sender uses the recipient's PUBLIC key to encrypt; the recipient uses their own PRIVATE key to decrypt — never share your private key
